question:
Let $C$ be the unit circle $x^2+y^2=1$. A point $p$ is chosen randomly
on the circumference $C$ and another point $q$ is chosen randomly from
the interior of $C$ (these points are chosen independently and
uniformly over their domains). Let $R$ be the rectangle with sides
parallel to the $x$ and $y$-axes with diagonal $pq$. What is the
probability that no point of $R$ lies outside of $C$?
solution:
Solution. Let \( p=(\cos \theta, \sin \theta) \) and \( q=(a, b) \). The other two vertices of \( R \) are \( (\cos \theta, b) \) and \( (a, \sin \theta) \). If \( |a| \leq|\cos \theta| \) and \( |b| \leq|\sin \theta| \), then each vertex \( (x, y) \) of \( R \) satisfies \( x^{2}+y^{2} \leq \cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta=1 \), and no points of \( R \) can lie outside of \( C \). Conversely, if no points of \( R \) lies outside of \( C \), then applying this to the two vertices other than \( p \) and \( q \), we find
\[
\cos ^{2} \theta+b^{2} \leq 1, \quad \text { and } \quad a^{2}+\sin ^{2} \theta \leq 1,
\]
or equivalently
\[
|b| \leq|\sin \theta|, \quad \text { and } \quad|a| \leq|\cos \theta| .
\]

These conditions imply that \( (a, b) \) lies inside or on \( C \), so for any given \( \theta \), the probability that the random point \( q=(a, b) \) satisfies \( (1) \) is
\[
\frac{2|\cos \theta| \cdot 2|\sin \theta|}{\pi}=\frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 \theta)|,
\]
and the overall probability is
\[
\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 \theta)| d \theta=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin (2 \theta) d \theta=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}}
\]

descriptive_long.question: Let $unitcircle$ be the unit circle $horizcoor^2+vertcoor^2=1$. A point $boundarypoint$ is chosen randomly on the circumference $unitcircle$ and another point $interiorpoint$ is chosen randomly from the interior of $unitcircle$ (these points are chosen independently and uniformly over their domains). Let $rectregion$ be the rectangle with sides parallel to the $horizcoor$ and $vertcoor$-axes with diagonal $boundarypoint interiorpoint$. What is the probability that no point of $rectregion$ lies outside of $unitcircle$?
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( boundarypoint=(\cos anglevar, \sin anglevar) \) and \( interiorpoint=(abscissa, ordinate) \). The other two vertices of \( rectregion \) are \( (\cos anglevar, ordinate) \) and \( (abscissa, \sin anglevar) \). If \( |abscissa| \leq|\cos anglevar| \) and \( |ordinate| \leq|\sin anglevar| \), then each vertex \( (horizcoor, vertcoor) \) of \( rectregion \) satisfies \( horizcoor^{2}+vertcoor^{2} \leq \cos ^{2} anglevar+\sin ^{2} anglevar=1 \), and no points of \( rectregion \) can lie outside of \( unitcircle \). Conversely, if no points of \( rectregion \) lies outside of \( unitcircle \), then applying this to the two vertices other than \( boundarypoint \) and \( interiorpoint \), we find\[\cos ^{2} anglevar+ordinate^{2} \leq 1, \quad \text { and } \quad abscissa^{2}+\sin ^{2} anglevar \leq 1,\]or equivalently\[|ordinate| \leq|\sin anglevar|, \quad \text { and } \quad|abscissa| \leq|\cos anglevar| .\]

These conditions imply that \( (abscissa, ordinate) \) lies inside or on \( unitcircle \), so for any given \( anglevar \), the probability that the random point \( interiorpoint=(abscissa, ordinate) \) satisfies \( (1) \) is\[\frac{2|\cos anglevar| \cdot 2|\sin anglevar|}{\pi}=\frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 anglevar)|,\]and the overall probability is\[\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 anglevar)| d anglevar=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin (2 anglevar) d anglevar=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}}\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $caterpillar$ be the unit circle $lighthouse^2+sailcloth^2=1$. A point $windstorm$ is chosen randomly
on the circumference $caterpillar$ and another point $butterfly$ is chosen randomly from
the interior of $caterpillar$ (these points are chosen independently and
uniformly over their domains). Let $stonework$ be the rectangle with sides
parallel to the $lighthouse$ and $sailcloth$-axes with diagonal $windstorm butterfly$. What is the
probability that no point of $stonework$ lies outside of $caterpillar$?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( windstorm=(\cos anchorage, \sin anchorage) \) and \( butterfly=(pineapple, carpenter) \). The other two vertices of \( stonework \) are \( (\cos anchorage, carpenter) \) and \( (pineapple, \sin anchorage) \). If \( |pineapple| \leq|\cos anchorage| \) and \( |carpenter| \leq|\sin anchorage| \), then each vertex \( (lighthouse, sailcloth) \) of \( stonework \) satisfies \( lighthouse^{2}+sailcloth^{2} \leq \cos ^{2} anchorage+\sin ^{2} anchorage=1 \), and no points of \( stonework \) can lie outside of \( caterpillar \). Conversely, if no points of \( stonework \) lies outside of \( caterpillar \), then applying this to the two vertices other than \( windstorm \) and \( butterfly \), we find
\[
\cos ^{2} anchorage+carpenter^{2} \leq 1, \quad \text { and } \quad pineapple^{2}+\sin ^{2} anchorage \leq 1,
\]
or equivalently
\[
|carpenter| \leq|\sin anchorage|, \quad \text { and } \quad|pineapple| \leq|\cos anchorage| .
\]

These conditions imply that \( (pineapple, carpenter) \) lies inside or on \( caterpillar \), so for any given \( anchorage \), the probability that the random point \( butterfly=(pineapple, carpenter) \) satisfies \( (1) \) is
\[
\frac{2|\cos anchorage| \cdot 2|\sin anchorage|}{\pi}=\frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 anchorage)|,
\]
and the overall probability is
\[
\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 anchorage)| d anchorage=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin (2 anchorage) d anchorage=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Let $massivesquare$ be the unit circle $verticalcoord^2+horizontalcoord^2=1$. A point $nonpoint$ is chosen randomly on the circumference $massivesquare$ and another point $exteriorpoint$ is chosen randomly from the interior of $massivesquare$ (these points are chosen independently and uniformly over their domains). Let $ovalshape$ be the rectangle with sides parallel to the $verticalcoord$ and $horizontalcoord$-axes with diagonal $nonpoint exteriorpoint$. What is the probability that no point of $ovalshape$ lies outside of $massivesquare$?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( nonpoint=(\cos straightvalue, \sin straightvalue) \) and \( exteriorpoint=(noncoordinate, antislope) \). The other two vertices of \( ovalshape \) are \( (\cos straightvalue, antislope) \) and \( (noncoordinate, \sin straightvalue) \). If \( |noncoordinate| \leq|\cos straightvalue| \) and \( |antislope| \leq|\sin straightvalue| \), then each vertex \( (verticalcoord, horizontalcoord) \) of \( ovalshape \) satisfies \( verticalcoord^{2}+horizontalcoord^{2} \leq \cos ^{2} straightvalue+\sin ^{2} straightvalue=1 \), and no points of \( ovalshape \) can lie outside of \( massivesquare \). Conversely, if no points of \( ovalshape \) lies outside of \( massivesquare \), then applying this to the two vertices other than \( nonpoint \) and \( exteriorpoint \), we find
\[
\cos ^{2} straightvalue+antislope^{2} \leq 1, \quad \text { and } \quad noncoordinate^{2}+\sin ^{2} straightvalue \leq 1,
\]
or equivalently
\[
|antislope| \leq|\sin straightvalue|, \quad \text { and } \quad|noncoordinate| \leq|\cos straightvalue| .
\]

These conditions imply that \( (noncoordinate, antislope) \) lies inside or on \( massivesquare \), so for any given \( straightvalue \), the probability that the random point \( exteriorpoint=(noncoordinate, antislope) \) satisfies \( (1) \) is
\[
\frac{2|\cos straightvalue| \cdot 2|\sin straightvalue|}{\pi}=\frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 straightvalue)|,
\]
and the overall probability is
\[
\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 straightvalue)| d straightvalue=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin (2 straightvalue) d straightvalue=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}}
\]

garbled_string.question:
Let $vtxqspae$ be the unit circle $lugwzpro^2+hjfsqnev^2=1$. A point $kmculfow$ is chosen randomly
on the circumference $vtxqspae$ and another point $nytahsej$ is chosen randomly from
the interior of $vtxqspae$ (these points are chosen independently and
uniformly over their domains). Let $ygralmiv$ be the rectangle with sides
parallel to the $lugwzpro$ and $hjfsqnev$-axes with diagonal $kmculfownytahsej$. What is the
probability that no point of $ygralmiv$ lies outside of $vtxqspae$?
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( kmculfow=(\cos gvrtmaji, \sin gvrtmaji) \) and \( nytahsej=(rqxumbae, snzlfkic) \). The other two vertices of \( ygralmiv \) are \( (\cos gvrtmaji, snzlfkic) \) and \( (rqxumbae, \sin gvrtmaji) \). If \( |rqxumbae| \leq|\cos gvrtmaji| \) and \( |snzlfkic| \leq|\sin gvrtmaji| \), then each vertex \( (lugwzpro, hjfsqnev) \) of \( ygralmiv \) satisfies \( lugwzpro^{2}+hjfsqnev^{2} \leq \cos ^{2} gvrtmaji+\sin ^{2} gvrtmaji=1 \), and no points of \( ygralmiv \) can lie outside of \( vtxqspae \). Conversely, if no points of \( ygralmiv \) lies outside of \( vtxqspae \), then applying this to the two vertices other than \( kmculfow \) and \( nytahsej \), we find
\[
\cos ^{2} gvrtmaji+snzlfkic^{2} \leq 1, \quad \text { and } \quad rqxumbae^{2}+\sin ^{2} gvrtmaji \leq 1,
\]
or equivalently
\[
|snzlfkic| \leq|\sin gvrtmaji|, \quad \text { and } \quad|rqxumbae| \leq|\cos gvrtmaji| .
\]

These conditions imply that \( (rqxumbae, snzlfkic) \) lies inside or on \( vtxqspae \), so for any given \( gvrtmaji \), the probability that the random point \( nytahsej=(rqxumbae, snzlfkic) \) satisfies \( (1) \) is
\[
\frac{2|\cos gvrtmaji| \cdot 2|\sin gvrtmaji|}{\pi}=\frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 gvrtmaji)|,
\]
and the overall probability is
\[
\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{2}{\pi}|\sin (2 gvrtmaji)| d gvrtmaji=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin (2 gvrtmaji) d gvrtmaji=\frac{4}{\pi^{2}}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let
\[
E=\Bigl\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb{R}^{3}\;:\;\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}+z^{2}\le 1\Bigr\}
\]
be the solid ellipsoid with semi-axes $3$, $2$, $1$ aligned with the coordinate axes.  

Choose angles
\[
\theta\sim\mathrm{Unif}[0,\pi],\qquad 
\varphi\sim\mathrm{Unif}[0,2\pi),\qquad 
\theta,\varphi\ \text{independent},
\]
and put the (surface) point
\[
p=(x_{0},y_{0},z_{0})
      =(3\sin\theta\cos\varphi,\;2\sin\theta\sin\varphi,\;\cos\theta)\in\partial E .
\]
Independently choose
\[
q=(a,b,c)
\]
uniformly from the interior of $E$ (with respect to volume measure).

Let $B$ be the axis-parallel rectangular box whose two opposite vertices are $p$ and $q$.

(a)  Find the probability $P$ that the whole box $B$ is contained in $E$.

(b)  Given that $B\subset E$, compute 
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\mid B\subset E\right].
\]

(c)  Given that $B\subset E$, compute 
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\Sigma(B)\mid B\subset E\right],
\]
where $\Sigma(B)$ denotes the total length of the $12$ edges of $B$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 -  A correct containment criterion  
Write $p=(x_{0},y_{0},z_{0})$ and $q=(a,b,c)$.  
Because $B$ is axis-parallel, every vertex $v=(x',y',z')$ of $B$ is obtained by independently choosing
\[
x'\in\{x_{0},a\},\qquad y'\in\{y_{0},b\},\qquad z'\in\{z_{0},c\}.
\]

($\Longrightarrow$)  Assume $B\subset E$.  
If, say, $\lvert a\rvert>\lvert x_{0}\rvert$, then the vertex $v=(a,y_{0},z_{0})$ of $B$ satisfies
\[
\frac{a^{2}}{9}+\frac{y_{0}^{2}}{4}+z_{0}^{2}>\frac{x_{0}^{2}}{9}+\frac{y_{0}^{2}}{4}+z_{0}^{2}=1,
\]
because the left-hand side increases strictly with $\lvert x\rvert $.  Hence $v\notin E$, contradicting $B\subset E$.  
Therefore $\lvert a\rvert\le\lvert x_{0}\rvert$.  
Exactly the same argument with the other coordinates yields
\[
\lvert a\rvert\le\lvert x_{0}\rvert,\qquad
\lvert b\rvert\le\lvert y_{0}\rvert,\qquad
\lvert c\rvert\le\lvert z_{0}\rvert .
\tag{1}
\]

($\Longleftarrow$)  Conversely, suppose (1) holds.  
For any vertex $v=(x',y',z')$ of $B$ we then have
\[
\frac{x'^{2}}{9}\le\frac{x_{0}^{2}}{9},\;
\frac{y'^{2}}{4}\le\frac{y_{0}^{2}}{4},\;
z'^{2}\le z_{0}^{2},
\]
so that
\[
\frac{x'^{2}}{9}+\frac{y'^{2}}{4}+z'^{2}
\;\le\;
\frac{x_{0}^{2}}{9}+\frac{y_{0}^{2}}{4}+z_{0}^{2}
=\;1.
\]
Thus every vertex of $B$ (and hence all of $B$) lies in $E$; i.e. $B\subset E$.  
We have proved
\[
B\subset E\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \text{the three inequalities in (1)}.
\]

Step 2 -  Probability of containment for fixed $p$  
For fixed $p$, the admissible points $q$ form the rectangular box
\[
R(p)=\{(a,b,c):\lvert a\rvert\le\lvert x_{0}\rvert,\;
                       \lvert b\rvert\le\lvert y_{0}\rvert,\;
                       \lvert c\rvert\le\lvert z_{0}\rvert\},
\]
whose volume is
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R(p))=8\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert .
\]
Since $R(p)\subset E$, the conditional probability is
\[
\mathbb{P}(B\subset E\mid p)=
\frac{\operatorname{Vol}(R(p))}{\operatorname{Vol}(E)}
    =\frac{8\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert}{\tfrac{4}{3}\pi\cdot3\cdot2\cdot1}
    =\frac{\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert}{\pi}.
\tag{2}
\]

Step 3 -  Expectation of $\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert$  
Because $\theta,\varphi$ are independent and uniform,
\[
x_{0}=3\sin\theta\cos\varphi,\quad
y_{0}=2\sin\theta\sin\varphi,\quad
z_{0}=\cos\theta .
\]
Hence
\[
\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert
      =6\lvert\sin^{2}\theta\cos\theta\sin\varphi\cos\varphi\rvert .
\]
With joint density $(2\pi^{2})^{-1}$,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert\bigr]
&=\frac{6}{2\pi^{2}}
  \int_{0}^{2\pi}\lvert\sin\varphi\cos\varphi\rvert\,d\varphi
  \int_{0}^{\pi}\lvert\sin^{2}\theta\cos\theta\rvert\,d\theta  \\
&=\frac{6}{2\pi^{2}}\cdot2\cdot\frac{2}{3}
  \;=\;\frac{4}{\pi^{2}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Step 4 -  Answer to part (a)  
Using (2),
\[
P=\mathbb{E}_{p}\bigl[\mathbb{P}(B\subset E\mid p)\bigr]
  =\frac{1}{\pi}\,\mathbb{E}\bigl[\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert\bigr]
  =\frac{4}{\pi^{3}} .
\]

Step 5 -  Conditional expectation of $\operatorname{Vol}(B)$  
For fixed $p$ and $B\subset E$, $(a,b,c)$ is uniform on $R(p)$, so
\[
\mathbb{E}[\,\lvert x_{0}-a\rvert\mid p,B\subset E]=\lvert x_{0}\rvert,\quad
\mathbb{E}[\,\lvert y_{0}-b\rvert\mid p,B\subset E]=\lvert y_{0}\rvert,\quad
\mathbb{E}[\,\lvert z_{0}-c\rvert\mid p,B\subset E]=\lvert z_{0}\rvert .
\]
Thus
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\mid p,B\subset E\bigr]
      =\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert .
\tag{3}
\]

Unconditioning and using (2)-(3),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\,1_{B\subset E}\bigr]
      &=\mathbb{E}_{p}\!\left[
        \mathbb{P}(B\subset E\mid p)\,
        \lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert
        \right]\\
      &=\frac{1}{\pi}\,
        \mathbb{E}\bigl[(x_{0}y_{0}z_{0})^{2}\bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
Compute the second moment:
\[
(x_{0}y_{0}z_{0})^{2}
      =36\sin^{4}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\varphi\sin^{2}\varphi .
\]
Using
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi}\sin^{4}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta=\frac{\pi}{16},\qquad
\int_{0}^{2\pi}\cos^{2}\varphi\sin^{2}\varphi\,d\varphi=\frac{\pi}{4},
\]
we find
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(x_{0}y_{0}z_{0})^{2}\bigr]
    =\frac{1}{2\pi^{2}}\cdot36\cdot\frac{\pi}{16}\cdot\frac{\pi}{4}
    =\frac{9}{32}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\mid B\subset E\right]
   =\frac{\dfrac{9}{32\pi}}{P}
   =\frac{9\pi^{2}}{128}.
\]

Step 6 -  Conditional expectation of the total edge length  
For fixed $p$,
\[
\Sigma(B)=4\bigl(\lvert x_{0}-a\rvert+\lvert y_{0}-b\rvert+\lvert z_{0}-c\rvert\bigr),
\]
so
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\Sigma(B)\mid p,B\subset E\bigr]
         =4\bigl(\lvert x_{0}\rvert+\lvert y_{0}\rvert+\lvert z_{0}\rvert\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\Sigma(B)\,1_{B\subset E}\bigr]
   =\frac{4}{\pi}\;
     \mathbb{E}
        \Bigl[\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert
              \bigl(\lvert x_{0}\rvert+\lvert y_{0}\rvert+\lvert z_{0}\rvert\bigr)\Bigr].
\]

Because of symmetry,
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[X^{2}YZ\bigr]
      =6/\pi^{2},\quad
\mathbb{E}\bigl[XY^{2}Z\bigr]
      =4/\pi^{2},\quad
\mathbb{E}\bigl[XYZ^{2}\bigr]
      =\frac{3}{4\pi},
\]
with $X=\lvert x_{0}\rvert$, $Y=\lvert y_{0}\rvert$, $Z=\lvert z_{0}\rvert$.  
Adding these expectations gives
\[
\mathbb{E}
   \Bigl[\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert
         \bigl(\lvert x_{0}\rvert+\lvert y_{0}\rvert+\lvert z_{0}\rvert\bigr)\Bigr]
   =\frac{10}{\pi^{2}}+\frac{3}{4\pi}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\Sigma(B)\mid B\subset E\right]
  =\frac{\dfrac{4}{\pi}\Bigl(\dfrac{10}{\pi^{2}}+\dfrac{3}{4\pi}\Bigr)}{P}
  =10+\frac{3\pi}{4}.
\]

\bigskip
Final answers:
\[
\boxed{P=\dfrac{4}{\pi^{3}}},\qquad
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\mid B\subset E]=\dfrac{9\pi^{2}}{128}},\qquad
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[\Sigma(B)\mid B\subset E]=10+\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}.
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Dimension raised from 2 D to 3 D: containment must be checked for a rectangular parallelepiped inside a solid ellipsoid.  
• Three independent angular and Cartesian integrations replace a single one; Beta–functions, trigonometric identities and multi–variable expectations are required.  
• Parts (b) and (c) involve conditional expectations that demand careful separation of the randomness of p and q, higher mixed moments (second and third), and deft use of symmetry.  
• The final answers are non-elementary constants (e.g. 9π²/128), showing that mere pattern spotting from the planar case is impossible.  
• The solution chain uses geometry, measure theory, probability, multivariate calculus and special-function identities—considerably deeper than in the original or the prior kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let
\[
E=\Bigl\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb{R}^{3}\;:\;\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}+z^{2}\le 1\Bigr\}
\]
be the solid ellipsoid with semi-axes $3$, $2$, $1$ aligned with the coordinate axes.  

Choose angles
\[
\theta\sim\mathrm{Unif}[0,\pi],\qquad 
\varphi\sim\mathrm{Unif}[0,2\pi),\qquad 
\theta,\varphi\ \text{independent},
\]
and put the (surface) point
\[
p=(x_{0},y_{0},z_{0})
      =(3\sin\theta\cos\varphi,\;2\sin\theta\sin\varphi,\;\cos\theta)\in\partial E .
\]
Independently choose
\[
q=(a,b,c)
\]
uniformly from the interior of $E$ (with respect to volume measure).

Let $B$ be the axis-parallel rectangular box whose two opposite vertices are $p$ and $q$.

(a)  Find the probability $P$ that the whole box $B$ is contained in $E$.

(b)  Given that $B\subset E$, compute 
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\mid B\subset E\right].
\]

(c)  Given that $B\subset E$, compute 
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\Sigma(B)\mid B\subset E\right],
\]
where $\Sigma(B)$ denotes the total length of the $12$ edges of $B$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 -  A correct containment criterion  
Write $p=(x_{0},y_{0},z_{0})$ and $q=(a,b,c)$.  
Because $B$ is axis-parallel, every vertex $v=(x',y',z')$ of $B$ is obtained by independently choosing
\[
x'\in\{x_{0},a\},\qquad y'\in\{y_{0},b\},\qquad z'\in\{z_{0},c\}.
\]

($\Longrightarrow$)  Assume $B\subset E$.  
If, say, $\lvert a\rvert>\lvert x_{0}\rvert$, then the vertex $v=(a,y_{0},z_{0})$ of $B$ satisfies
\[
\frac{a^{2}}{9}+\frac{y_{0}^{2}}{4}+z_{0}^{2}>\frac{x_{0}^{2}}{9}+\frac{y_{0}^{2}}{4}+z_{0}^{2}=1,
\]
because the left-hand side increases strictly with $\lvert x\rvert $.  Hence $v\notin E$, contradicting $B\subset E$.  
Therefore $\lvert a\rvert\le\lvert x_{0}\rvert$.  
Exactly the same argument with the other coordinates yields
\[
\lvert a\rvert\le\lvert x_{0}\rvert,\qquad
\lvert b\rvert\le\lvert y_{0}\rvert,\qquad
\lvert c\rvert\le\lvert z_{0}\rvert .
\tag{1}
\]

($\Longleftarrow$)  Conversely, suppose (1) holds.  
For any vertex $v=(x',y',z')$ of $B$ we then have
\[
\frac{x'^{2}}{9}\le\frac{x_{0}^{2}}{9},\;
\frac{y'^{2}}{4}\le\frac{y_{0}^{2}}{4},\;
z'^{2}\le z_{0}^{2},
\]
so that
\[
\frac{x'^{2}}{9}+\frac{y'^{2}}{4}+z'^{2}
\;\le\;
\frac{x_{0}^{2}}{9}+\frac{y_{0}^{2}}{4}+z_{0}^{2}
=\;1.
\]
Thus every vertex of $B$ (and hence all of $B$) lies in $E$; i.e. $B\subset E$.  
We have proved
\[
B\subset E\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \text{the three inequalities in (1)}.
\]

Step 2 -  Probability of containment for fixed $p$  
For fixed $p$, the admissible points $q$ form the rectangular box
\[
R(p)=\{(a,b,c):\lvert a\rvert\le\lvert x_{0}\rvert,\;
                       \lvert b\rvert\le\lvert y_{0}\rvert,\;
                       \lvert c\rvert\le\lvert z_{0}\rvert\},
\]
whose volume is
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(R(p))=8\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert .
\]
Since $R(p)\subset E$, the conditional probability is
\[
\mathbb{P}(B\subset E\mid p)=
\frac{\operatorname{Vol}(R(p))}{\operatorname{Vol}(E)}
    =\frac{8\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert}{\tfrac{4}{3}\pi\cdot3\cdot2\cdot1}
    =\frac{\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert}{\pi}.
\tag{2}
\]

Step 3 -  Expectation of $\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert$  
Because $\theta,\varphi$ are independent and uniform,
\[
x_{0}=3\sin\theta\cos\varphi,\quad
y_{0}=2\sin\theta\sin\varphi,\quad
z_{0}=\cos\theta .
\]
Hence
\[
\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert
      =6\lvert\sin^{2}\theta\cos\theta\sin\varphi\cos\varphi\rvert .
\]
With joint density $(2\pi^{2})^{-1}$,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert\bigr]
&=\frac{6}{2\pi^{2}}
  \int_{0}^{2\pi}\lvert\sin\varphi\cos\varphi\rvert\,d\varphi
  \int_{0}^{\pi}\lvert\sin^{2}\theta\cos\theta\rvert\,d\theta  \\
&=\frac{6}{2\pi^{2}}\cdot2\cdot\frac{2}{3}
  \;=\;\frac{4}{\pi^{2}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Step 4 -  Answer to part (a)  
Using (2),
\[
P=\mathbb{E}_{p}\bigl[\mathbb{P}(B\subset E\mid p)\bigr]
  =\frac{1}{\pi}\,\mathbb{E}\bigl[\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert\bigr]
  =\frac{4}{\pi^{3}} .
\]

Step 5 -  Conditional expectation of $\operatorname{Vol}(B)$  
For fixed $p$ and $B\subset E$, $(a,b,c)$ is uniform on $R(p)$, so
\[
\mathbb{E}[\,\lvert x_{0}-a\rvert\mid p,B\subset E]=\lvert x_{0}\rvert,\quad
\mathbb{E}[\,\lvert y_{0}-b\rvert\mid p,B\subset E]=\lvert y_{0}\rvert,\quad
\mathbb{E}[\,\lvert z_{0}-c\rvert\mid p,B\subset E]=\lvert z_{0}\rvert .
\]
Thus
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\mid p,B\subset E\bigr]
      =\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert .
\tag{3}
\]

Unconditioning and using (2)-(3),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\,1_{B\subset E}\bigr]
      &=\mathbb{E}_{p}\!\left[
        \mathbb{P}(B\subset E\mid p)\,
        \lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert
        \right]\\
      &=\frac{1}{\pi}\,
        \mathbb{E}\bigl[(x_{0}y_{0}z_{0})^{2}\bigr].
\end{aligned}
\]
Compute the second moment:
\[
(x_{0}y_{0}z_{0})^{2}
      =36\sin^{4}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\varphi\sin^{2}\varphi .
\]
Using
\[
\int_{0}^{\pi}\sin^{4}\theta\cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta=\frac{\pi}{16},\qquad
\int_{0}^{2\pi}\cos^{2}\varphi\sin^{2}\varphi\,d\varphi=\frac{\pi}{4},
\]
we find
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(x_{0}y_{0}z_{0})^{2}\bigr]
    =\frac{1}{2\pi^{2}}\cdot36\cdot\frac{\pi}{16}\cdot\frac{\pi}{4}
    =\frac{9}{32}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\mid B\subset E\right]
   =\frac{\dfrac{9}{32\pi}}{P}
   =\frac{9\pi^{2}}{128}.
\]

Step 6 -  Conditional expectation of the total edge length  
For fixed $p$,
\[
\Sigma(B)=4\bigl(\lvert x_{0}-a\rvert+\lvert y_{0}-b\rvert+\lvert z_{0}-c\rvert\bigr),
\]
so
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\Sigma(B)\mid p,B\subset E\bigr]
         =4\bigl(\lvert x_{0}\rvert+\lvert y_{0}\rvert+\lvert z_{0}\rvert\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\Sigma(B)\,1_{B\subset E}\bigr]
   =\frac{4}{\pi}\;
     \mathbb{E}
        \Bigl[\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert
              \bigl(\lvert x_{0}\rvert+\lvert y_{0}\rvert+\lvert z_{0}\rvert\bigr)\Bigr].
\]

Because of symmetry,
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[X^{2}YZ\bigr]
      =6/\pi^{2},\quad
\mathbb{E}\bigl[XY^{2}Z\bigr]
      =4/\pi^{2},\quad
\mathbb{E}\bigl[XYZ^{2}\bigr]
      =\frac{3}{4\pi},
\]
with $X=\lvert x_{0}\rvert$, $Y=\lvert y_{0}\rvert$, $Z=\lvert z_{0}\rvert$.  
Adding these expectations gives
\[
\mathbb{E}
   \Bigl[\lvert x_{0}y_{0}z_{0}\rvert
         \bigl(\lvert x_{0}\rvert+\lvert y_{0}\rvert+\lvert z_{0}\rvert\bigr)\Bigr]
   =\frac{10}{\pi^{2}}+\frac{3}{4\pi}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\Sigma(B)\mid B\subset E\right]
  =\frac{\dfrac{4}{\pi}\Bigl(\dfrac{10}{\pi^{2}}+\dfrac{3}{4\pi}\Bigr)}{P}
  =10+\frac{3\pi}{4}.
\]

\bigskip
Final answers:
\[
\boxed{P=\dfrac{4}{\pi^{3}}},\qquad
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Vol}(B)\mid B\subset E]=\dfrac{9\pi^{2}}{128}},\qquad
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[\Sigma(B)\mid B\subset E]=10+\dfrac{3\pi}{4}}.
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Dimension raised from 2 D to 3 D: containment must be checked for a rectangular parallelepiped inside a solid ellipsoid.  
• Three independent angular and Cartesian integrations replace a single one; Beta–functions, trigonometric identities and multi–variable expectations are required.  
• Parts (b) and (c) involve conditional expectations that demand careful separation of the randomness of p and q, higher mixed moments (second and third), and deft use of symmetry.  
• The final answers are non-elementary constants (e.g. 9π²/128), showing that mere pattern spotting from the planar case is impossible.  
• The solution chain uses geometry, measure theory, probability, multivariate calculus and special-function identities—considerably deeper than in the original or the prior kernel variant.
